questions.q: You spend an hour vibe coding a personal cron that scrapes a weather page every morning. Is that the right tool?
choices: No — vibe coding is always wrong.
Yes — it's a personal tool, low stakes, you'll throw it away if it breaks.
No — anything automated should be built with Claude Code.
It depends on which model you used.
questions.explain: Vibe coding is exactly right for low-stakes personal automations you'll throw away. The bar is "looks right, runs once, don't care if it breaks." Real coding would be overkill.

questions.q: Short answer: what is the single artifact that lets you switch from vibe coding to real coding mid-project?
questions.explain: A PRD (Product Requirements Document) — even a one-paragraph PRD. It captures intent so the harness can build against something specific instead of filling in gaps with its own assumptions.

questions.q: You vibe coded a feature, it works on your machine, and the team wants to ship it next week. What is the right next step?
choices: Open Claude Code or Kilo Code, write a one-paragraph PRD, rebuild the feature with tests, read every diff.
Just deploy the prototype and see if anyone complains.
Rewrite the whole project from scratch in a different language.
questions.explain: The vibe-then-real pattern: keep the prototype as exploration, then rebuild the production version with a coding harness, a PRD, and tests. This is the most common shipping pattern in 2026.

questions.q: Per the source quote, why does vibe coding break on drag-handlers?
choices: Vibe coding with intent-free prompts fills in gaps with assumptions, and those assumptions are often wrong on edge cases.
Models can't generate event handlers at all.
questions.explain: Vibe coding produces code that "looks right" but reasons about the wrong model of the problem. Drag-handlers are full of edge cases the AI fills in with guesses if you don't specify them in a PRD.

questions.q: True or false: real coding means writing the code by hand without AI help.
questions.explain: False. Real coding means using AI as a tool — usually a coding harness — while you drive the verification (PRD, tests, code review). The AI still writes most of the code. You just run the loop.

questions.q: Which is the better production stack for an internal tool that only your team uses: a coding harness with tests, or Hermes with a vibe-coded skill?
choices: Either is fine, depending on the team — both can be production-grade for internal tools.
Neither — internal tools should be hand-coded.
questions.explain: Internal tools have low blast radius. A Hermes-scheduled vibe-coded skill is often the right answer (see Hermes L01). A coding-harness version with tests is also fine. The wrong answer is the one that costs more than the tool is worth.
